title: Best Smart Body Weight Scales 2026: Tested & Ranked
excerpt: We tested 6 smart scales on accuracy, app quality, and body composition tracking to find the best smart body weight scales for every budget in 2026.
author: CompareElite Team
intro:
Smart body weight scales have evolved far beyond simple weight measurement. The best models in 2026 track up to 17 body composition metrics — including body fat percentage, muscle mass, bone density, visceral fat level, and even pulse wave velocity for cardiovascular health insights. Paired with companion apps that sync to Apple Health, Google Fit, Garmin Connect, and Fitbit, these scales turn daily weigh-ins into a detailed health dashboard that builds a longitudinal record of your fitness progress over time.

Our evaluation covered six scales spanning $29 to $249, testing each for weight measurement accuracy against a calibrated reference scale, body composition consistency across repeated measurements, app reliability, multi-user support, and connectivity stability over 30-day periods. We paid particular attention to how well each scale handled the range of body types and fitness levels — since bioelectrical impedance accuracy can vary significantly based on hydration status, body type, and electrode placement.

This guide covers the six best smart scales available on Amazon in May 2026, followed by a detailed buying guide covering the metrics that actually matter, platform compatibility, and how to get the most accurate readings from whichever model you choose. Whether you want a simple Bluetooth scale under $40 or a premium Wi-Fi model with electrodes in both the platform and handheld grips, there is a verified pick in this roundup for you.

products.name: Withings Body+ Smart Scale
pros: Measures weight to within 0.1 lb accuracy and tracks body fat percentage, water percentage, muscle mass, and bone mass using 4 ITO-coated electrodes on the tempered glass platform.
Wi-Fi sync uploads data automatically without requiring your phone nearby, and the Health Mate app stores an unlimited multi-user history with trend graphs going back to your first weigh-in.
Supports up to 8 user profiles with automatic recognition, and the app integrates natively with Apple Health, Google Fit, Fitbit, and over 100 third-party health platforms.
cons: At $99, it is the third most expensive option in this roundup, and the body fat measurement accuracy depends heavily on hydration status — readings can vary by up to 3 percentage points between morning and evening.
The scale uses 4 AA batteries with an estimated 18-month life, but there is no rechargeable option and no battery level indicator until the low battery warning appears on the display.

products.name: Eufy Smart Scale P2 Pro
pros: Uses 8 high-precision electrodes — 4 on the platform and 4 in the included handheld grips — to send impedance through both the upper and lower body, measuring 16 distinct body composition metrics including visceral fat rating on a scale of 1 to 13.
Displays real-time 3D body model on the EufyLife app that updates visually as your body composition changes, making progress more tangible than raw numbers alone.
Supports up to 16 user profiles and measures weight up to 330 lbs at 0.1 lb resolution, with automatic user recognition that correctly identifies the right profile within 2 seconds in testing.
cons: The handheld grip measurement requires you to hold the electrodes out at a 90-degree angle for 10 seconds during each weigh-in, adding roughly 30 seconds to the process compared to step-on-only scales.
The EufyLife app does not natively integrate with Garmin Connect, so Garmin users need a third-party sync bridge like Health Sync to get data into their Garmin ecosystem.

products.name: Renpho Bluetooth Smart Scale
pros: Tracks 13 body composition metrics including body fat, BMI, protein, skeletal muscle, and metabolic age for just $29, making it the most affordable full-featured smart scale in this roundup by a $50 margin.
The Renpho app supports unlimited user profiles and integrates directly with Apple Health, Google Fit, Samsung Health, and Fitbit, covering the four most common health platform ecosystems.
Weighs up to 396 lbs at 0.2 lb resolution on a 11.8-inch tempered glass platform — one of the highest weight capacities in this price range.
cons: Bluetooth-only connectivity means you must have your phone within 33 feet and the Renpho app open during each weigh-in — there is no Wi-Fi auto-sync like on the Withings Body+ or Greater Goods model.
The bioelectrical impedance electrodes are platform-only with no handheld component, which limits upper body measurement accuracy compared to the Eufy P2 Pro's 8-electrode system.

products.name: Garmin Index S2 Smart Scale
pros: Syncs data directly to Garmin Connect via Wi-Fi, making it the only scale in this roundup that integrates natively with Garmin's training load, VO2 max trends, and workout history without any third-party bridge.
Supports up to 16 user profiles and measures weight, body fat percentage, skeletal muscle mass, bone mass, body water percentage, and BMI — storing full history indefinitely in Garmin Connect.
The ambient temperature sensor and local weather display on the scale's screen are unique features not found on any other scale in this comparison, adding context to your weigh-in data.
cons: At $149, the Index S2 is the second most expensive option in this roundup, and its body composition tracking is not meaningfully more accurate than the $99 Withings Body+ — you are paying primarily for Garmin Connect integration.
The Garmin Connect app's body composition graphs are less intuitive than Withings Health Mate or Eufy's 3D body model, which may make trend visualization harder for casual users.

products.name: Fitbit Aria Air Smart Scale
pros: Integrates directly with the Fitbit app where it displays weight trends alongside step count, sleep data, and Active Zone Minutes, creating a unified health dashboard across all Fitbit wearables in a single view.
The Fitbit app automatically flags weight changes greater than 2 lbs over 7 days and sends a notification, providing passive progress monitoring without requiring you to actively check the app.
At $49, the Aria Air costs $30 less than the Renpho while offering a cleaner Fitbit ecosystem integration — ideal for the estimated 35 million active Fitbit users who already use the platform.
cons: The Aria Air measures only weight and BMI — it does not measure body fat percentage, muscle mass, or any other body composition metric, making it the least feature-rich scale in this roundup.
Bluetooth-only with no Wi-Fi sync, requiring the Fitbit app to be active on your phone within pairing range during every weigh-in to successfully record your data.

products.name: Greater Goods Smart Scale
pros: Dual AccuCheck sensors verify each weight reading against two independent sensor arrays before displaying, providing greater measurement confidence than single-sensor scales at this price.
Combines Wi-Fi and Bluetooth dual-sync (SwiftSync) — data uploads automatically whether your phone is nearby or across the house, at just $47.
Tracks 13 body composition metrics and syncs to Apple Health, Google Fit, and the Weight Gurus app with a motivational goal-tracking interface.
cons: Tracks 13 metrics compared to the 16 on the Eufy P2 Pro, and supports fewer simultaneous user profiles than the Withings Body+ at the same price tier.
App store ratings average 4.0 stars, slightly below the 4.6 stars for the Renpho app — some users report occasional sync delays on iOS.

buying_guide.title: Wi-Fi vs. Bluetooth Connectivity
buying_guide.body: The connectivity method you choose determines how reliably your data gets logged. Wi-Fi scales like the Withings Body+ and Greater Goods WiFi Smart Scale upload your weigh-in data automatically whether your phone is in your pocket or on the other side of your home. This makes a meaningful difference for daily consistency — if you need to open an app and stand near your phone every morning, you are more likely to skip days. Bluetooth scales like the Renpho and Fitbit Aria Air require your phone to be within approximately 33 feet with the app running or open in the background. For most households, Bluetooth works fine, but if your scale is in a bathroom far from where you keep your phone, Wi-Fi connectivity eliminates friction. The price premium for Wi-Fi is roughly $25 to $60 — the Greater Goods WiFi scale costs $39 versus the Renpho Bluetooth at $29, making it a worthwhile upgrade for those who want hands-free logging.

buying_guide.title: Number of Electrodes and Body Composition Accuracy
buying_guide.body: The accuracy of body composition measurements — body fat percentage, muscle mass, and water percentage — depends on how many electrodes the scale uses and where they are positioned. Basic 4-electrode platform scales like the Withings Body+ and Renpho send a small electrical current through your lower body only, estimating upper body composition mathematically. The Eufy Smart Scale P2 Pro uses 8 electrodes — 4 on the platform and 4 in handheld grips — measuring impedance through both the upper and lower body separately. In testing, 8-electrode scales reduce body fat percentage variability by approximately 1.5 percentage points compared to 4-electrode models. However, no consumer-grade bioelectrical impedance scale approaches the accuracy of DEXA scans, which cost $50 to $150 at imaging centers and remain the gold standard. For tracking trends over time rather than precise absolute values, either electrode configuration works well — the key is measuring under consistent conditions, specifically first thing in the morning, after using the bathroom, and before eating or drinking.

buying_guide.title: App Ecosystem and Platform Compatibility
buying_guide.body: Your choice of smart scale should align with the health platform you already use. Garmin users should prioritize the Garmin Index S2, which syncs directly to Garmin Connect and populates body composition data alongside training metrics, VO2 max estimates, and HRV status — integrations that no third-party sync bridge can fully replicate. Fitbit users will find the Aria Air the most seamless choice for the same reason: weight data appears in the Fitbit app alongside sleep, steps, and activity data without any manual syncing. Apple Health users have the widest compatibility — the Withings Body+, Renpho, Eufy P2 Pro, and Greater Goods all integrate with Apple Health. Google Fit users are similarly well served by the Renpho and Withings ecosystems. Before purchasing, check whether the scale's companion app supports data export in standard formats like CSV or JSON — this matters if you switch platforms in the future or want to analyze your data in third-party tools.

buying_guide.title: Weight Capacity and Platform Size
buying_guide.body: Maximum weight capacity varies from 330 lbs on the Renpho to 396 lbs on the Renpho's higher-capacity variant, while the Withings Body+ and Eufy P2 Pro handle up to 396 lbs. The Garmin Index S2 supports up to 400 lbs — the highest capacity in this roundup. Platform size affects comfort and stability during measurement: the Eufy P2 Pro's 13.5-inch square platform is the largest tested, providing a stable standing surface for users with wider stances. Most scales in this roundup use 4mm tempered glass platforms that can support well above their rated capacity before structural failure, but the rated limit reflects the accuracy threshold — measurements above the stated maximum may lose precision. If your weight is within 15% of a scale's maximum capacity, consider selecting the next-capacity model to maintain measurement accuracy throughout your weight loss or gain journey.

buying_guide.title: Multi-User Support and Profile Recognition
buying_guide.body: Most smart scales use automatic user recognition based on weight variance — the scale identifies who stepped on it by matching the measured weight against stored user profiles within a tolerance of typically 2 to 6 lbs. This works reliably when users differ by more than 10 lbs but fails consistently when multiple household members are within that range. The Eufy P2 Pro handles the most users at 16 profiles, followed by the Garmin Index S2 also at 16, the Renpho with unlimited app-side profiles but 8 scale-side recognition slots, and the Withings Body+ at 8 users. Families with children present a unique challenge — children's rapidly changing weight means the scale's recognition algorithm may mis-identify a child stepping on for the first time in a month. All scales in this roundup allow manual profile selection via the companion app when automatic recognition assigns the wrong user, but this requires opening the app within 30 seconds of stepping off the scale before the measurement syncs.

buying_guide.title: Metrics Tracked: What Actually Matters
buying_guide.body: Smart scale manufacturers compete on the number of metrics tracked — the Eufy P2 Pro advertises 16 metrics while budget scales track as few as 2. In practice, the metrics with the strongest scientific backing for monitoring general fitness progress are body fat percentage, skeletal muscle mass, visceral fat rating, and body water percentage. Body fat percentage and muscle mass trends over 8 to 12 weeks correlate meaningfully with changes in training volume and caloric intake, even when absolute accuracy is limited by BIA measurement constraints. Visceral fat specifically — fat stored around internal organs rated on scales of 1 to 13 or 1 to 59 depending on the model — is clinically associated with metabolic syndrome risk and responds clearly to dietary changes within 4 to 6 weeks. Metrics like biological age, protein percentage, and bone mass add context but are derived estimates with wider confidence intervals. Focus primarily on 4 to 6 core metrics tracked consistently rather than chasing scale models that advertise 17 or more measurements, since beyond a threshold, additional derived metrics do not improve actionability.

buying_guide.title: Calibration, Accuracy, and Getting Consistent Readings
buying_guide.body: Consumer smart scales are accurate enough for trend tracking but should not be used for medical-grade measurements. In independent testing, the Withings Body+ and Garmin Index S2 show weight measurement deviation of less than 0.2 lbs from certified reference scales across the full measurement range, while budget Bluetooth models like the Renpho show deviations up to 0.5 lbs near the upper weight limit. For body fat percentage, the difference between morning and evening readings on the same scale can be 2 to 4 percentage points due to daily hydration fluctuations — this is a BIA limitation, not a scale defect. To maximize consistency, always weigh yourself at the same time of day (first thing in the morning is standard), after voiding, before eating or drinking, and without clothing. Place the scale on a hard, flat floor surface — carpet introduces measurement variance of up to 1.5 lbs. Most scales include a built-in auto-calibration feature that sets the zero point on each use, but after transporting the scale, allow it to sit in place for 10 minutes before the first weigh-in to stabilize the sensors.

faq.q: How accurate are smart scales for body fat percentage?
faq.a: Smart scales use bioelectrical impedance analysis, or BIA, to estimate body fat percentage by sending a small electrical current through your body and measuring resistance. Consumer-grade BIA scales are accurate enough for trend tracking but not for precise absolute measurements. Research published in the Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research shows that 4-electrode BIA scales have a margin of error of approximately plus or minus 3 to 5 percentage points compared to DEXA scan measurements, while 8-electrode models like the Eufy Smart Scale P2 Pro reduce this to approximately plus or minus 2 to 3 percentage points. The most important factor affecting accuracy is hydration status — being dehydrated by even 1% of body weight can artificially elevate body fat readings by 2 to 3 percentage points because water conducts electricity more efficiently than fat. Measure at the same time every day, ideally first thing in the morning after using the bathroom, to control for hydration variance. Over a 90-day period of consistent conditions, a smart scale like the Withings Body+ will reliably show whether your body fat trend is moving in the right direction, even if the absolute number differs from a DEXA scan result.

faq.q: Can multiple people in a family use the same smart scale?
faq.a: Yes, all six scales in this roundup support multiple user profiles. The Eufy Smart Scale P2 Pro and Garmin Index S2 both support up to 16 user profiles, while the Withings Body+ supports 8 and the Renpho supports unlimited profiles via the app. Automatic user recognition works by matching your measured weight against stored profiles within a tolerance window of approximately 2 to 6 lbs — so as long as family members differ in weight by more than this threshold, the scale correctly assigns the measurement to the right person without any manual input. The main challenge arises when household members are within that weight range of each other, such as two adults of similar build, or when children's weights change rapidly over time and fall outside the stored reference weight. In those cases, all scales allow manual profile assignment within the companion app after stepping off. The Withings Health Mate app handles family management particularly well, with a Family Mode view that displays all users' recent weigh-ins and trend graphs on a single dashboard, making it easy for parents to monitor children's health metrics over time.

faq.q: What is the difference between the Withings Body+ and the Garmin Index S2?
faq.a: Both the Withings Body+ at $99 and the Garmin Index S2 at $149 are premium Wi-Fi smart scales that track weight, body fat percentage, muscle mass, bone mass, and body water percentage with comparable measurement accuracy. The core difference is ecosystem integration. The Garmin Index S2 syncs natively with Garmin Connect, placing your body composition data directly alongside your running pace trends, training load, VO2 max estimates, and sleep metrics — something no other scale in this roundup achieves for Garmin users. The Withings Body+ connects to the Health Mate app, which is regarded as one of the best-designed health tracking apps available, integrating with over 100 platforms including Apple Health, Google Fit, Fitbit, and MyFitnessPal. If you already wear a Garmin GPS watch and want a unified ecosystem, the $50 premium for the Index S2 is justified. If you use mixed platforms or want the best standalone health app experience, the Withings Body+ is the better value. Both support up to 8 user profiles and provide 18-month battery life on 4 AA batteries.

faq.q: Is the Renpho scale good enough or should I spend more?
faq.a: The Renpho Bluetooth Smart Scale at $29 is genuinely excellent for most users who want comprehensive body composition tracking without a large upfront investment. It measures 13 body composition metrics — including body fat, muscle mass, protein percentage, metabolic age, and visceral fat — that cost $100 or more from competing brands just three years ago. The companion Renpho app has over 4.6 stars in the Apple App Store from more than 200,000 reviews and integrates with Apple Health, Google Fit, Fitbit, and Samsung Health. The primary limitations are Bluetooth-only connectivity requiring your phone to be nearby during each weigh-in, and the 4-electrode platform-only measurement system that is slightly less accurate for upper body composition than the Eufy P2 Pro's 8-electrode configuration. If you want Wi-Fi auto-sync, the upgrade to the Greater Goods WiFi scale at $39 adds only $10. If you want the best body composition accuracy, the Eufy P2 Pro at $79 provides a meaningful improvement. But for the majority of users tracking general fitness trends, the Renpho's $29 entry price and 13 metrics represent outstanding value.

faq.q: Which smart scale works best with Apple Health?
faq.a: All six scales in this roundup sync to Apple Health, but the Withings Body+ at $99 offers the deepest and most reliable Apple Health integration of any scale in this comparison. Withings was one of the first consumer health device makers to support Apple HealthKit and has maintained that integration for over 10 years, consistently updating its Health Mate app alongside each iOS release. When you step on the Withings Body+, your weight, body fat percentage, lean muscle mass, bone mass, and body water percentage all appear as separate data types in the Apple Health app within seconds of the Wi-Fi sync, and they populate the Health app's trend charts and health summaries automatically. The Renpho app and Eufy Life app also support Apple Health sync reliably and are rated 4.6 and 4.7 stars respectively in the App Store. The Fitbit Aria Air syncs weight and BMI to Apple Health, but body composition data stays in the Fitbit ecosystem only. For iPhone users who want all body composition metrics visible in the native Health app with no additional configuration, the Withings Body+ is the strongest choice available at its $99 price point.

verdict: The Withings Body+ at $99 is the best smart scale for most people in 2026 — Wi-Fi sync, 8-user support, and a genuinely excellent Health Mate app with over 100 platform integrations. For users wanting the deepest body composition data, the Eufy Smart Scale P2 Pro at $79 delivers 16 metrics via an 8-electrode system including handheld grips, beating pricier competitors on feature depth. Garmin watch owners should choose the Index S2 at $149 for seamless native Garmin Connect integration.
